title: Capstone: Task Tracker CLI
description: Build a command-driven task tracker that parses lines like `add`, `done`, and `list` — combining lists of dicts, string parsing, conditionals, and per-command functions.
objectives: Represent state as a list of dictionaries
Dispatch commands with conditionals and per-command functions
Print aligned, formatted output with f-strings

completionRules.expected:
1. [ ] Buy groceries
2. [x] Finish report
3. [ ] Call dentist
Done: 1/3

tests.name: done_task marks the correct task done

recap: You turned lines of input into state changes and formatted output — the core loop of every CLI tool. Lists of dicts, one function per command, and a simple dispatch `if/elif` is enough to build real programs.
practicePrompts: Add a `delete <id>` command that removes a task from the list.
Add a `pending` command that lists only unfinished tasks.
Show the task text in uppercase for done tasks, lowercase for pending.

practiceExercises.title: Count pending tasks
practiceExercises.prompt: Given the task list below, print `Pending: N` where N is the number of tasks where `done` is False. Expected output: `Pending: 2`.
practiceExercises.goal: Filter a list of dicts by a boolean field.
practiceExercises.starterCode:
tasks = [
    {"id": 1, "text": "A", "done": True},
    {"id": 2, "text": "B", "done": False},
    {"id": 3, "text": "C", "done": False},
]

def pending(tasks):
    # Return the count of tasks where done is False
    pass

print(f"Pending: {pending(tasks)}")
hints: Loop and increment a counter when `not t["done"]`.
Or use `sum(1 for t in tasks if not t["done"])` if you're comfortable with that.

practiceExercises.title: Toggle a task
practiceExercises.prompt: Write `toggle(tasks, task_id)` that flips `done` on the task with the given id. Call it with id=1, then print each task as `{id}. [x|' '] {text}`. Expected output: `1. [ ] A\n2. [ ] B`.
practiceExercises.goal: Find an item by id, mutate it, then format each item.
practiceExercises.starterCode:
tasks = [
    {"id": 1, "text": "A", "done": True},
    {"id": 2, "text": "B", "done": False},
]

def toggle(tasks, task_id):
    # Flip the done flag on the matching task
    pass

toggle(tasks, 1)
for t in tasks:
    mark = "x" if t["done"] else " "
    print(f'{t["id"]}. [{mark}] {t["text"]}')
hints: Loop: `for t in tasks: if t["id"] == task_id: ...`.
Inside the match, `t["done"] = not t["done"]` flips the flag.
You don't need to return anything — the list is mutated in place.

practiceExercises.title: First pending task
practiceExercises.prompt: Given the task list below, print the text of the FIRST pending task (lowest id where done is False). Expected output: `First pending: B`.
practiceExercises.goal: Short-circuit a search through a list of dicts.
practiceExercises.starterCode:
tasks = [
    {"id": 1, "text": "A", "done": True},
    {"id": 2, "text": "B", "done": False},
    {"id": 3, "text": "C", "done": False},
]

def first_pending(tasks):
    # Return the text of the first task with done=False, or None
    pass

print(f"First pending: {first_pending(tasks)}")
hints: Loop in order; return as soon as you find `not t["done"]`.
If nothing matches, fall through and return None.
